<b>Berinert</b> (C1-INH [human])<sup>1</:
• Recommended dose is 20 IU per kg patient’s body weight, administered by slow IV injection at a rate of ~4 mL/min
  • Doses <20 IU/kg should not be administered
  • Appropriately trained patients may self-administer upon recognition of symptoms of an HAE attack
<b>Kalbitor</b> (ecallantide)<sup>2</sup:
• Recommended dose is 30 mg (3 mL) administered SC in three 10 mg (1 mL) injections
  • If attack persists, an additional dose of 30 mg may be administered within a 24-hour period
  • Kalbitor should only be administered by an HCP with appropriate medical support to manage anaphylaxis and HAE
<b>Firazyr</b> (icatibant)<sup>3</sup>:
• Recommended dose is 30 mg administered by SC injection in the abdominal area
  • If response is inadequate or symptoms recur, additional doses can be administered at intervals of ≥6 hours
  • Maximum of 3 doses in any 24-hour period
  • Appropriately trained patients may self-administer upon recognition of symptoms of an HAE attack
<b>Ruconest</b> (C1-INH [recombinant])<s:
• Recommended dose is 50 U per kg, with a maximum of 4200 U, administered by slow IV injection over ~5 minutes
  • If attack symptoms persist, a second dose can be administered at the recommended dose level (not to exceed 4200 U per dose)
  • Maximum of 2 doses in any 24-hour period
  • Appropriately trained patients may self-administer upon recognition of symptoms of an HAE attack
<b>Ekterly</b> (sebetralstat)<sup>5</sup:
• Recommended dose is 600 mg (2 tablets) administered orally at the earliest recognition of an acute HAE attack
  • If response is inadequate or symptoms worsen or recur, a second dose can be taken at least 3 hours after the first dose
  • Maximum of 1200 mg (4 tablets) in any 24-hour period
<b>Generic</b> (danazol)<sup>7</sup>:
• Dosage individualized based on patient clinical response
  • Recommended initial dose is 200 mg orally 2-3 times a day
  • After a favorable initial response, proper continuing dose should be determined by decreasing dosage by ≤50% at intervals of 1-3 months or longer if frequency of attacks prior to treatment dictates
  • If an attack occurs, daily dosage may be increased up to 200 mg
<b>Cinryze</b> (C1-INH [human] IV)<sup>8:
For IV use only

Patients ≥12 years of age:
  • Initial dose of 1000 IU every 3 or 4 days
  • If not responding adequately, doses up to 2000 IU (not exceeding 80 IU/kg) every 3 or 4 days may be considered
  • Infusion rate of 1 mL/min over 10 minutes

Patients 6-11 years of age:
  • Initial dose of 500 IU every 3 or 4 days
  • Dose may be adjusted to response, up to 1000 IU every 3 or 4 days
  • Infusion rate of 1 mL/min over 5 minutes

Appropriately trained patients may self-administer Cinryze
<b>Haegarda</b> (C1-INH [human] SC)<sup>:
• Recommended dose is 60 IU per kg body weight by SC injection every 3 or 4 days
  • Appropriately trained patients may self-administer Haegarda

  • Anaphylaxis has been reported after administration of Kalbitor

<b>Generic</b> (danazol)<sup>7</sup>:
WARNING:

PREGNANCY
  • Use in pregnancy is contraindicated
  • Sensitive pregnancy test is recommended prior to starting therapy
  • Nonhormonal contraception method should be used during therapy
  • If patient becomes pregnant, danazol should be discontinued and the patient apprised of potential risk to the fetus

THROMBOEMBOLIC EVENTS
  • Thromboembolism, thrombotic and thrombophlebitic events including sagittal sinus thrombosis and life-threatening or fatal strokes have been reported

HEPATIC DYSFUNCTION
  • Peliosis hepatis and benign hepatic adenoma have been observed with long-term use
  • Peliosis hepatis and hepatic adenoma may be silent until complicated by potentially life-threatening intraabdominal hemorrhage
  • Attempts should be made to determine the lowest dose that will provide adequate protection
  • If treatment was initiated at a time of exacerbation of hereditary angioneurotic edema due to trauma, stress or other cause, periodic attempts to decrease or withdraw therapy should be considered

INCREASED INTRACRANIAL PRESSURE
  • Danazol is associated with benign intracranial hypertension
  • Patients with signs and symptoms of this condition should be screened for papilledema and, if present, advised to discontinue danazol immediately and referred to a neurologist for further diagnosis and care

<b>Berinert</b> (C1-INH [human])<sup>1</:
Hypersensitivity
  • Severe hypersensitivity reactions may occur
  • Epinephrine should be immediately available to treat severe hypersensitivity reactions following discontinuation of administration

Thromboembolic Events
  • Serious arterial and venous TEEs have occurred after administration of recommended dose of plasma-derived C1-INH in patients with HAE
  • TEEs have also been reported following administration of C1-INH when used for unapproved indications at higher than recommended doses
  • Benefits of treatment should be weighed against risks of TEEs in patients with known risk factors
  • Patients with known risk factors for TEEs should be monitored during and after Berinert administration

Transmissible Infectious Agents
  • Because Berinert is made from human blood, it may carry a risk of transmitting infectious agents (eg, viruses, the vCJD agent, and, theoretically, the CJD agent)

Laryngeal HAE Attacks
  • Patients should be advised to seek immediate medical attention in an appropriate healthcare facility following self-administration for treatment of laryngeal HAE attacks
<b>Kalbitor</b> (ecallantide)<sup>2</sup:
Hypersensitivity Reactions, Including Anaphylaxis
  • Potentially serious hypersensitivity reactions, including anaphylaxis, have occurred in patients treated with Kalbitor
  • In 255 patients with HAE treated with Kalbitor in clinical trials, 4% experienced anaphylaxis
  • Kalbitor should be administered in a setting equipped to manage anaphylaxis and HAE
  • Given the similarity in hypersensitivity symptoms and HAE symptoms, patients should be monitored closely for hypersensitivity reactions
  • Kalbitor is contraindicated in patients with known clinical hypersensitivity to the product
<b>Firazyr</b> (icatibant)<sup>3</sup>:
Laryngeal Attacks
  • Patients should be advised to seek immediate medical attention in an appropriate healthcare facility following treatment of laryngeal attacks with Firazyr

Thromboembolic Events

• A temporary decrease in HDL and possibly increased LDL has been reported during danazol therapy; these alterations may be marked, and HCPs should consider the potential impact on the risk of atherosclerosis and coronary artery disease in accordance with the potential benefit of therapy to the patient
  • Patients should be watched closely for signs of androgenic effects, some of which may not be reversible despite drug discontinuation
  • Because danazol may cause fluid retention, conditions that might be influenced by this factor require careful observation; should be used with caution in patients with diabetes mellitus
  • Periodic liver function tests should be performed because hepatic dysfunction manifested by increases in serum transaminase levels has been reported with danazol
  • Danazol has been reported to cause exacerbation of the manifestations of acute intermittent porphyria
  • Laboratory monitoring of the hematologic state should be considered

Hypersensitivity Reactions

Thromboembolic Events

<b>Haegarda</b> (C1-INH [human] SC)<sup>:
Hypersensitivity Reactions
  • Severe hypersensitivity reactions may occur
  • If severe hypersensitivity occurs, Haegarda should be discontinued and appropriate treatment instituted
  • Epinephrine should be immediately available to treat severe hypersensitivity reactions

Thromboembolic Events
  • At the recommended dose, a causal relationship between TEEs and Haegarda has not been established
  • However, thrombosis has occurred with off-label use of high doses of C1-INH IV for prevention or therapy of capillary leak syndrome before, during, or after cardiac surgery

Transmissible Infectious Agents
  • Because Haegarda is made from human blood, it may carry a risk of transmitting infectious agents (eg, viruses, vCJD agent and, theoretically, CJD agent)

<b>Berinert</b> (C1-INH [human])<sup>1</:
In a post-marketing study that monitored for antibodies to C1-INH among 46 patients with HAE-C1INH-Type 1 or -Type 2 HAE at Day 1 (first HAE attack requiring Berinert 20 IU/kg IV, baseline value), and at 3, 6, and 9 months:
  • No patients developed inhibitory antibodies to C1-INH at any time point
  • 28.2% of patients had detectable levels of noninhibitory antibodies at some point during the study, including 19.6% who had detectable noninhibitory antibodies at baseline
  • The presence of noninhibitory anti-C1-INH antibodies did not induce apparent immunologically associated clinical events

Because the incidence of antibody formation highly depends on the sensitivity and specificity of the assay used and several other factors, it may be misleading to compare the incidence of antibodies to Berinert in the studies described above with the incidence of antibodies in other studies or to other products
<b>Kalbitor</b> (ecallantide)<sup>2</sup:
There is potential for immunogenicity with use of Kalbitor

In the Kalbitor HAE program:
  • Patients developed antibodies to Kalbitor
  • Rates of seroconversion increased with exposure to Kalbitor over time
  • Overall, 20.2% of patients seroconverted to anti-ecallantide antibodies
  • Neutralizing antibodies to ecallantide were determined in vitro to be present in 8.8% of patients and were not associated with loss of efficacy

Patients who seroconvert may be at a higher risk of hypersensitivity reaction

The long-term effects of antibodies to Kalbitor are not known

Because the incidence of antibody formation highly depends on the sensitivity and specificity of the assay used and several other factors, it may be misleading to compare the incidence of antibodies to Kalbitor with the incidence of antibodies to other products
<b>Firazyr</b> (icatibant)<sup>3</sup>:
Across repeated treatment in controlled trials:
  • 4 patients tested positive for anti-icatibant antibodies, of whom 3 had subsequent tests that were negative
  • No hypersensitivity or anaphylactic reactions were reported with Firazyr
  • No association between anti-icatibant antibodies and efficacy was observed
<b>Ruconest</b> (C1-INH [recombinant])<s:
There is potential for immunogenicity with use of Ruconest

When pre- and post-exposure samples from 205 HAE patients treated for 650 attacks with Ruconest were tested for antibodies against C1-INH or rhC1-INH and for antibodies against HRIs, and testing was performed prior to and after treatment of a first attack and subsequent repeated attacks at 7, 22 or 28, and 90 days after Ruconest treatment:
  • Prior to Ruconest exposure, the frequency of anti-C1-INH antibodies varied from 1.2% to 1.6% of samples
  • After first exposure, the frequency of anti-C1-INH antibodies varied from 0.6% to 1.0% of samples
  • After repeated exposures, the frequency of anti-C1-INH antibodies varied from 0.5 to 2.2% of samples
  • The frequency of anti-HRI antibodies was 1.0% in pre-exposure samples, and after the first exposure varied from 3.5% to 4.6%
  • After repeated exposure, the frequency of anti-HRI antibodies varied from 5.7% to 17% of samples
  • At least 10% of patients formed a specific antibody response to Ruconest after 5 treated attacks
  • No anti-C1-INH neutralizing antibodies were detected
  • Observed anti-C1-INH and anti-HRI antibodies were not associated with adverse clinical findings

Because the incidence of antibody formation highly depends on the sensitivity and specificity of the assay used and several other factors, comparison of the incidence of antibodies to Ruconest with the incidence of antibodies to other products may be misleading

<b>Takhzyro</b> (lanadelumab-flyo)<sup>1:
Because the incidence of ADAs highly depends on the sensitivity, specificity, and methodology of the assay used, meaningful comparisons of ADA incidence in the studies described below with ADA incidence in other studies, including those of lanadelumab-flyo or other lanadelumab products, cannot be made

In Trial 1 with patients ≥12 years of age:
  • 10 patients (12%) receiving lanadelumab-flyo and 2 patients (5%) receiving placebo had ≥1 ADA-positive sample in the 26-week treatment period
  • Antibody titers were low
  • ADA response was transient in 2 of 10 lanadelumab-flyo-treated patients and 1 of 2 placebo-treated patients
  • Pre-existing low titer antibodies were observed in 3 lanadelumab-flyo-treated patients and 1 placebo-treated patient with ADAs
  • 2 patients receiving 150 mg Q4W had low titer antibodies classified as neutralizing

In an open-label study of patients 2 to <12 years of age:
  • 3/20 (15%) of lanadelumab-treated patients who completed the study developed ADAs during the 52-week treatment period (all were 6 to <12 years of age)
  • The ADA response was transient in all 3 patients
  • None had pre-existing antibodies
  • 1 patient had neutralizing antibodies
  • ADA development, including neutralizing antibodies against lanadelumab-flyo, did not appear to adversely affect PK, PD, safety, or clinical response

In OASIS-HAE with patients ≥12 years of age receiving Dawnzera for up to 24 weeks:
  • 20% (9/45) of patients on 80 mg Q4W and 22% (5/23) on 80 mg Q8W had treatment-emergent ADAs

In the OASIS-HAE OLE in which patients continued treatment with Dawnzera 80 mg Q4W or Q8W for up to 3 years (median exposure: 227 days):
  • 35% (22/63) of patients on 80 mg Q4W had treatment-emergent ADAs, including patients initially randomized to Dawnzera 80 mg Q4W in OASIS-HAE (36%) and patients initially randomized to placebo in OASIS-HAE (32%)
  • 21% (3/14) of patients who received 80 mg Q8W in both OASIS-HAE and OLE had treatment-emergent ADAs

In general, ADA development was not found to affect PD, safety, or efficacy of Dawnzera

An increase in donidalorsen plasma Ctrough was observed in ADA-positive patients with high titers

Because of small sample size, the effect of ADA on PK, PD, safety, and effectiveness of Dawnzera is inconclusive

Effect of Other Drugs on Ekterly
Strong and Moderate CYP3A4 Inhibitors:
  • Avoid use with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors
  • Patients taking moderate CYP3A4 inhibitors should take one Ekterly 300 mg dose; a second 300 mg dose may be taken at least 3 hours after the first dose if response is inadequate or if symptoms worsen or recur

CYP3A4 Inducers:
  • Avoid use with moderate or strong CYP3A4 inducers
<b>Generic</b> (danazol)<sup>7</sup>:
• Prolongation of prothrombin time occurs in patients stabilized on warfarin
  • Danazol may increase carbamazepine levels in patients taking both drugs
  • Danazol can cause insulin resistance; caution should be exercised when used with antidiabetic drugs
  • Danazol may raise plasma levels of cyclosporin and tacrolimus, leading to increased renal toxicity of these drugs; monitoring of concentrations of these drugs and appropriate dose adjustments may be needed when used with danazol
  • Danazol can increase the calcemic response to synthetic vitamin D analogues in primary hypoparathyroidism
  • The risk of myopathy and rhabdomyolysis is increased by the use of danazol with statins; caution should be exercised if used concomitantly; consult the statin product labeling for specific information on dose restrictions in the presence of danazol

Activity of Cinryze also demonstrated by increased plasma C4 levels (mean 8.1 mg/dL at baseline to 8.6 mg/dL 12 hours after infusion)

C4 levels were also increased in children 7-11 years of age treated with 500 U or 1000 U every 3-4 days for 12 weeks (mean 6.80 mg/dL at baseline to 7.92 mg/dL for 500 U, and 9.27 mg/dL for 1000 U, 1 hour after final dose)
<b>Haegarda</b> (C1-INH [human] SC)<sup>:
Dose-dependent increases in plasma C1-INH levels occur following Haegarda administration

Subsequent increases in C4 levels (to within normal range) observed after Haegarda 60 IU/kg administration
<b>Takhzyro</b> (lanadelumab-flyo)<sup>1:
Concentration-dependent inhibition of plasma kallikrein demonstrated following use of Takhzyro at therapeutic doses

in adult and pediatric patients 12 to <18 years of age with HAE

For pediatric patients 2 to <6 years of age (150 mg Q4W) and 6 to <12 years (150 mg Q2W), the observed mean percent change from baseline cleaved HMWK levels was similar to that in adult and pediatric patients 12 to <18 years (300 mg Q2W or Q4W)

Takhzyro did not prolong the QT/QTc interval
<b>Orladeyo</b> (berotralstat)<sup>11</s:
Concentration-dependent inhibition of plasma kallikrein demonstrated following administration of Orladeyo once daily in patients with HAE

Clinically significant QTc interval prolongation not observed following MRD in adults; at 3 × the MRD, mean increase in QTcF was 15.9 msec, and the

increase was concentration dependent
<b>Andembry</b> (garadacimab-gxii)<sup>1:
Concentration-dependent inhibition of FXIIa-mediated kallikrein activity

observed in patients with HAE

Inhibition of kallikrein activity observed after the first dose
<b>Dawnzera</b><sup>TM</sup> (donidalors:
Following 80 mg Q4W or Q8W in adult and pediatric patients ≥12 years of age with HAE:
  • A decrease in plasma prekallikrein observed at first assessment (Week 4), with mean percentage reduction of 48% from baseline for both doses
  • Mean percentage reductions in trough plasma prekallikrein from baseline to Week 24 were 73% and 47% following 80 mg Q4W and Q8W, respectively

Clinically significant QTc interval prolongation not observed following the MRD

<b>Berinert</b> (C1-INH [human])<sup>1</:
Following one 500-1500 IU dose in adults and children with HAE, mean PK parameters (adjusted vs a baseline attack-free period) were:
  • Half-life = 18.4 hours
  • Clearance = 1.44 mL/hr/kg
  • Volume steady state = 35.4 mL/kg
  • Mean residence time = 26.4 hours

Following a 15 IU/kg dose, AUC = 12.8 hr*IU/mL

Specific Populations

In 5 pediatric patients 6-13 years of age, the half-life was shorter and clearance (per kg basis) was faster compared to adults; the clinical implication of this difference is unknown

Studies have not evaluated PK by gender, race, geriatric age, or the presence of renal or hepatic impairment
<b>Kalbitor</b> (ecallantide)<sup>2</sup:
Following one 30 mg dose to healthy participants, mean PK parameters were:
  • Cmax = 586 ng/mL ~2-3 hours post-dose
  • AUC = 3017 ng*hour/mL
  • Half-life = 2.0 hours
  • Clearance = 153 mL/min
  • Volume of distribution = 26.4 L

Specific Populations

Patient body weight, age, and gender were not found to significantly affect Kalbitor exposure

No PK data are available in patients or participants with hepatic or renal impairment
<b>Firazyr</b> (icatibant)<sup>3</sup>:
PK profile of Firazyr is similar in patients with HAE and healthy participants

Following one 30 mg dose, mean PK parameters were:
  • Cmax = 974 ng/mL after ~0.75 hours
  • Mean AUC = 2165 ng*hr/mL
  • Half-life = 1.4 hours
  • Clearance = 245 mL/min
  • Volume steady state = 29.0 L

Icatibant is not degraded by oxidative metabolic pathways, not an inhibitor of major CYP isoenzymes, and not an inducer of CYP 1A2 and 3A4

Specific Populations

Following one dose, PK were comparable in healthy participants and those with hepatic impairment

A renal impairment study was not conducted; renal impairment is not expected to affect PK

Age and gender effects on PK were observed, but dose adjustment is not warranted based on age or gender:
  • Older patients (>65 years) tend to have lower clearance and higher systemic exposure (AUC and Cmax) than younger patients (18-45 years)
  • Clearance is correlated with body weight; thus, female patients with typically lower body weights compared to male patients have lower clearance values, resulting in ~2-fold higher systemic exposure (AUC and Cmax)
<b>Ruconest</b> (C1-INH [recombinant])<s:
Following

Ruconest 50 U/kg in patients with asymptomatic HAE, mean PK parameters were:
  • Cmax = 1.2 U/mL
  • Tmax = 0.31 hours
  • AUC = 3.3 U*hr/mL
  • Half-life = 2.4 hours
  • Clearance = 1207 mL/hour
  • Volume steady state = 3.0 L

Clearance of Ruconest was nonlinear (decreased with increasing dose) over 25-100 U/kg dose range

Specific Populations

Studies have not evaluated PK by race, age, or the presence of renal or hepatic impairment
<b>Ekterly</b> (sebetralstat)<sup>5</sup:
Following one 600 mg dose in patients with HAE, PK parameters were:
  • Geometric mean Cmax = 6080 ng/mL
  • Mean AUC = 17,600 ng*hr/mL

Following a second 600 mg dose 3 hours after the first dose, Cmax increases 10% and AUC increases 90% when compared to data following a single dose

No clinically relevant differences in PK observed between healthy participants and patients with HAE

Following a single 600 mg dose:
  • Median Tmax = ~1 hour
  • Mean half-life = 5.3-8.9 hours
  • Estimated clearance = 30.7 L/hour
  • Estimated volume of distribution = 70.1 L

Sebetralstat is primarily metabolized by CYP3A4 and to a lesser extent by CYP2C8

Specific Populations

No clinically relevant differences in PK based on body weight, age, sex, race, or mild renal impairment (eGFR 60-89 mL/min/1.73 m2)

Effect of moderate and severe renal impairment (eGFR <60 mL/min/1.73 m2) on PK is unknown
<b>Generic</b> (danazol)<sup>7</sup>:
Following one 400 mg dose in healthy male volunteers, PK parameters were:

  • Mean half-life = 9.70 hours

Exposure (Cmax and AUC) is not dose proportional

Bioavailability, absorption, and exposure are affected by food intake

Can partition into cell membranes and likely

distributes into deep tissues
<b>Cinryze</b> (C1-INH [human] IV)<sup>8:
Following one 1000 U dose in patients

with nonsymptomatic HAE, mean PK parameters were:
  • Cmax = 0.68 U/mL
  • Tmax = 3.9 hours
  • AUC = 74.5 U*hr/mL
  • Half-life = 56 hours
  • Clearance = 0.85 mL/min

Exposure (Cmax and AUC) increased from a single to double dose of Cinryze, though it was not dose proportional

Specific Populations

In children 7-11 years of age administered 500 U or 1000 U every 3-4 days for 12 weeks, mean C1-INH functional activities were 0.53 U/mL, and 0.81 U/mL, respectively,

vs the baseline value 0.18 U/mL (1 hour after final dose)

After 1000 U dosing, mean exposure (AUC and Cmax) was ~30% higher in children 7-11 years of age than in adults

Studies have not evaluated PK based on gender, race, geriatric age, or the presence of renal or hepatic impairment
<b>Haegarda</b> (C1-INH [human] SC)<sup>:
Following 60 IU/kg twice weekly dosing, PK parameters were:
  • Mean clearance = 1.03 mL/hour/kg
  • Geometric mean Cmax = 60.7%
  • Median Tmax = 59 hours
  • Median half-life = 69 hours

Steady-state PK not dose proportional at 20-80 IU/kg in patients with HAE

Specific Populations

PK have not been evaluated by gender, race, or the presence of renal or hepatic impairment

Population analysis showed age did not significantly affect PK, but body weight–adjusted clearance was 10%-11% higher in children (8 to <12 years) and adolescents (12 to <18 years) compared to adults
<b>Takhzyro</b> (lanadelumab-flyo)<sup>1:
PK were approximately

dose-proportional in the therapeutic dose range in patients with HAE

Time to reach steady state concentration was ~70 days

Following one 300 mg Q2W dose, mean PK parameters were:
  • Cmax = 34.4 mcg/mL
  • Tmax = 4.11 days
  • AUC = 408 mcg*day/mL
  • Half-life = 15 days
  • Volume of distribution = 16.6 L
  • Clearance = 0.809 L/day

Specific Populations

Age, gender, and race did not meaningfully affect PK after correcting for body weight. Body weight affected clearance, distribution, and exposure (AUC and Cmax) in lighter patients, but dose adjustment is not required

Average concentration at steady state differed in pediatric and adult patients:
  • ~25% higher in patients 12 to <18 years on 300 mg Q2W vs adults on 300 mg Q2W
  • ~7% higher in patients 6 to <12 years on 150 mg Q2W vs adults on 300 mg Q2W

  • ~14% lower in patients 2 to <6 years on 150 mg Q4W vs adults on 300 mg Q2W

Based on population analysis, mild (eGFR 60-89 mL/min/1.73 m2) or moderate (30-59 mL/min/1.73 m2) renal impairment had no effect on clearance or volume of distribution
<b>Orladeyo</b> (berotralstat)<sup>11</s:
Exposure increases in a greater than dose-proportional manner and steady state is reached in ~6-12 days
  • After 150 mg once daily in adults, geometric mean Cmax = 158 ng/mL; AUC = 2770 ng*hr/mL
  • After 110 mg once daily, geometric mean Cmax is 97.8 ng/mL; AUC = 1600 ng*hr/mL

Median half-life is ~93 hours

PK similar in healthy adults and patients with HAE

Metabolized by CYP2D6 and CYP3A4

Specific Populations

In adults and pediatric patients 12 to <18 years of age, no significant differences in PK based on age, sex, race, and body weight

Based on population analyses, body weight affected systemic exposure. In patients 2 to <12 years of age at the recommended dosage, median Cmax is expected to be up to 24% higher compared to adults, though this difference is not considered clinically significant

Adults with severe renal impairment (CLCR <30 mL/min) treated with one dose 1.33 × the MRD had no clinically significant difference in exposure compared with patients with normal renal function (Cmax increased by 47%, AUC increased by 14%). PK have not been evaluated in patients with ESRD

PK were compared in adults with normal or impaired hepatic function following one 150 mg dose:
  • Mild impairment: PK unchanged
  • Moderate impairment: Had increased Cmax (by 77%), AUC (by 78%), and median half-life (by 37%)
  • Severe impairment: Had increased Cmax (by 27%) and median half-life (by 22%), and decreased AUC (by 5%)
<b>Andembry</b> (garadacimab-gxii)<sup>1:
Following one

200 mg SC dose to healthy volunteers, mean PK parameters were:
  • Cmax = 18.4 mcg/mL
  • AUC = 11470 mcg*hr/mL
  • Tmax = 6 days
  • Volume of distribution = 11.8 L
  • Half-life = 17.4 days
  • Clearance = 0.02 L/hour

PK similar between healthy volunteers and patients with HAE

Specific Populations

No clinically significant differences in PK observed based on age, gender, race, body weight, and

mild to moderate renal impairment (eGFR 30 to <90 mL/min/1.73 m2)

Effect of severe renal impairment (eGFR <30 mL/min/1.73 m2) on PK is unknown
<b>Dawnzera</b><sup>TM</sup> (donidalors:
Following multiple doses Q4W and/or Q8W, PK similar between healthy participants and patients with HAE

In healthy volunteers, AUC increased in a greater than dose-proportional manner (at 0.25 × MRD to 80 mg Q4W)

Following 80 mg Q4W in patients with HAE, geometric mean PK parameters were:
  • Cmax = 417 ng/mL
  • AUC = 5240 ng*hr/mL

Absorbed with median Tmax of ~2 hours post-dose

Half-life is ~1 month in a typical patient with HAE

Expected to distribute primarily to the liver and kidneys

The ASO is expected to be metabolized by nucleases within the liver. Based on in vitro studies, donidalorsen is not a substrate of CYP enzymes

The linker that connects the ASO to GalNAc residues undergoes dephosphorylation and oxidative metabolism to form inactive metabolites,

which are minimally released in circulation

Specific Populations

No clinically meaningful differences in PK or PD based on age, body weight, sex, race, ethnicity, disease status, mild renal impairment (eGFR ≥60 to <90 mL/min/1.73 m2), or mild hepatic impairment (total bilirubin ≤1 × ULN and AST >1 × ULN, or total bilirubin >1 to 1.5 × ULN and any AST)

Has not been evaluated in patients with moderate or severe renal impairment, ESRD, or moderate or severe hepatic impairment

<b>Berinert</b> (C1-INH [human])<sup>1</:
Efficacy of Berinert for treatment of acute abdominal or facial HAE attacks demonstrated in a placebo-controlled, double-blind, randomized, parallel-group, dose-finding, 3-arm clinical trial (N=124)

Berinert was also evaluated in uncontrolled OLE study at 15 centers in US and Canada, in patients who participated in the randomized controlled study, to assess those with any type of subsequent HAE attack (ie, abdominal, facial, peripheral, or laryngeal)

Randomized Controlled Trial

This trial enrolled adult and pediatric patients with C1-INH deficiency who experienced an acute moderate to severe attack of abdominal or facial HAE. Patient age ranged from 6-72 years, 67.7% were female, and ~90% were White

Patients received a single dose of Berinert 10 IU/kg body weight (n=39)a, Berinert 20 IU/kg (n=43), or placebo (n=42) within 5 hours of an attack. Patients who experienced no or insufficient relief of symptoms within 4 hours could be administered another dose of Berinert (20 IU/kg for placebo group, 10 IU/kg for 10 IU/kg group), or of placebo (for the 20 IU/kg group). If a patient developed life-threatening laryngeal edema, immediate start of open-label treatment with a 20 IU/kg body weight dose of Berinert was allowed

aAs Berinert 10 IU/kg is not a recommended dose for treatment of acute HAE attacks, these data are not shown herein.

Key endpoint:
  • Median time to onset of symptom relief: 48 minutes for Berinert 20 IU/kg vs >4 hours for placebo

Additional endpoints:
  • Proportion of patients with onset of symptom relief within 60 minutes after administration: 62.8% for Berinert 20 IU/kg vs 26.2% for placebo
  • Proportion of patients requiring additional rescue medication: 30.2% for Berinert 20 IU/kg vs 54.8% for placebo

Open-Label Extension Study
  • In OLE, 51 patients had 747 abdominal attacks, 21 had 51 facial attacks, 30 had 235 peripheral attacks, and 16 had 48 laryngeal attacks; some patients may have experienced attacks in more than one location
  • Median time to onset of symptom relief of laryngeal attacks: 0.25 hours (n=48)
  • Median time to complete symptom resolution of laryngeal attacks: 8.4 hours
<b>Kalbitor</b> (ecallantide)<sup>2</sup:
Efficacy of Kalbitor for treatment of acute HAE attacks in adolescents and adults evaluated in 2 randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled clinical trials:
  • EDEMA4 (N=96)
  • EDEMA3 (N=72)

In both trials, patients having an HAE attack, at any anatomic location, with ≥1 moderate or severe symptom, were randomized to Kalbitor 30 mg or placebo

Because patients could participate in both trials, a total of 143 unique patients participated, with a mean age of 36 years (range 11-77), 94 were female, and 123 were White. There were 64 patients with abdominal attacks, 55 with peripheral attacks, and 24 with laryngeal attacks

In both trials, the effects of Kalbitor were evaluated using MSCS (a 4-point measure of symptom severity, from “normal” to “severe”) and the TOS (a categorical scale from “significant improvement” to “significant worsening”) of an acute HAE attack

EDEMA4

Key endpoint:
  • Mean change from baseline in MSCS score at 4 hours: −0.8 for Kalbitor (n=47) vs −0.4 for placebo (n=42)

Additional endpoints:
  • Mean TOS at 4 hours: 53 for Kalbitor (n=47) vs 8 for placebo (n=42)
  • Mean change from baseline in MSCS score at 24 hours: −1.5 for Kalbitor vs −1.1 for placebo
  • Mean TOS at 24 hours: 89 for Kalbitor vs 55 for placebo
  • Proportion of patients requiring medical intervention to treat unresolved symptoms within 24 hours: 33% for Kalbitor vs 50% for placebo

EDEMA3

Key endpoint:
  • Mean TOS at 4 hours: 63 for Kalbitor (n=34) vs 36 for placebo (n=35)

Additional endpoints:
  • Mean change from baseline in MSCS score at 4 hours: −1.1 for Kalbitor vs −0.6 for placebo
  • Proportion of patients requiring medical intervention to treat unresolved symptoms within 24 hours: 14% for Kalbitor vs 36% for placebo
<b>Firazyr</b> (icatibant)<sup>3</sup>:
Efficacy of Firazyr for treatment of acute HAE attacks in 223 adults evaluated in 3 controlled clinical trials:
  • Trial 1, a randomized, placebo-controlled, double-blind, parallel-group trial
  • Second placebo-controlled trial
  • Active-controlled trial

Among the patients in these studies, mean age was 38 years, 64% were female, and 95% were White

In Trial 1 (N=98), patients with moderate to severe cutaneous or abdominal attacks or mild to moderate laryngeal attacks were randomized to Firazyr 30 mg or placebo. Patients with severe laryngeal attacks of HAE received open-label Firazyr 30 mg.

In the second placebo-controlled and an active-controlled trial, a total of 26 and 35 patients, respectively, received Firazyr 30 mg for an attack

Response to therapy was primarily assessed using VAS (100 mm scale, with higher scores indicating more severity) and patient- and physician-reported symptom scores for abdominal and

cutaneous pain and swelling

Trial 1

Key endpoint:
  • Assessed using 3-item composite VAS (averaged assessments of skin swelling, skin pain, and abdominal pain); response defined as ≥50% reduction from pretreatment 3-item VAS score
  • Median time to 50% reduction in symptoms of cutaneous or abdominal attacks: 2.0 hours for Firazyr (n=43) vs 19.8 hours for placebo (n=45)

Additional endpoints:
  • Median time to almost complete symptom relief (VAS <10 mm):

    8.0 hours for Firazyr vs 36.0 hours for placebo
  • Proportion requiring additional rescue medication: 7% for Firazyr vs 40% for placebo

Pooled Trial Data

Across 3 controlled trials, Firazyr-treated patients (N=223) had median time to 50% reduction from baseline symptoms ranging from 2.0 to 2.3 hours.

Recurrent attacks:
  • In all 3 trials, patients’ subsequent attacks could be treated in OLE, in which 225 patients were treated with 1076 doses of 30 mg Firazyr for 987 attacks
  • In an analysis of the first 5 attacks treated with Firazyr (621 doses for 582 attacks), median times to 50% symptom reduction were similar across attacks (2.0, 2.0, 2.4, 2.0, 1.5 hours)
  • 93% of attacks were treated with a single dose of Firazyr

Laryngeal attacks
  • 60 patients with laryngeal attacks were treated with Firazyr in controlled trials
  • Efficacy results similar to those for nonlaryngeal sites of attack

Open-Label Self-Administration Study
  • Self-administration of Firazyr was assessed in an open-label trial (N=56)
  • Patients who administered Firazyr during an acute HAE attack had a median time to 50% reduction from the pretreatment composite 3-item VAS score of 2.6 hours
<b>Ruconest</b> (C1-INH [recombinant])<s:
Efficacy of Ruconest for treatment of acute HAE attacks established in Study 1, a double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled trial, which included an OLE phase; and supported by results of 2 additional randomized, placebo-controlled trials and 2 additional OLE studies

Study 1

75 adult and adolescent patients with HAE were randomized to Ruconest 50 U/kg (n=44) or placebo (n=31). Rescue treatment with Ruconest was available for patients who did not experience onset of relief at 4 hours after study drug administration, or earlier for patients with life-threatening oropharyngeal-laryngeal symptoms. Patients ranged from 17-69 years of age, 63% were female, and 96% were White

Key endpoint:
  • Median time to onset of symptom relief, assessed using patient-reported responses to 2 questions from a TEQ (assessed the severity of symptoms at each affected anatomic location, using a 7-point scale [“much worse” to “much better”], and whether symptoms had begun to decrease notably since receiving the study medication [“yes” or “no”]); to achieve the primary endpoint, a patient had to have a positive response to both questions along with persistence of improvement at the next assessment time
  • Median time to onset of symptom relief: 90 minutes for Ruconest 50 U/kg vs 152 minutes for placebo

Additional endpoints:
  • Among patients achieving symptom relief within 4 hours, 3% in Ruconest group vs 27% in placebo group had a relapse in symptoms within 24 hours
  • Proportion of patients requiring rescue treatment: 11% for Ruconest 50 U/kg vs 42% for placebo

Study 1 Open-Label Extension Study
  • 44 patients who completed the controlled trial were enrolled into OLE and treated with Ruconest 50 U/kg for total of 170 attacks
  • Median time to onset of symptom relief: 75 minutes
  • 3% of attacks received a second Ruconest dose

Study 2 and Study 3
  • In Study 2 (North American randomized, placebo- controlled trial), patients received one dose of Ruconest 50 U/kg (n=12), Ruconest 100 U/kg (n=13), or placebo (n=13); patients ranged from 17-66 years of age, 74% were female, and 92% were White
  • In Study 3 (European randomized, placebo- controlled trial), patients received one dose of Ruconest 100 U/kg (n=16) or placebo (n=16); patients ranged from 17- 71 years of age, 53% were female, and 100% were White
  • In both studies, Ruconest had significantly shorter times to beginning of symptom relief based on VAS

Study 2 and Study 3 Open-Label Extension Study
  • 119 patients were treated with Ruconest for 362 acute attacks
  • Efficacy was maintained for repeat attacks
<b>Ekterly</b> (sebetralstat)<sup>5</sup:
Efficacy of Ekterly for treatment of acute HAE attacks in adults and pediatric patients ≥12 years of age evaluated in a double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled, crossover clinical trial (KONFIDENT)

Patients received Ekterly 600 mg, Ekterly 300 mgb, or placebo. If determined by the patient, a second dose could be administered after 3 hours. Patients were required to treat an eligible attack prior to crossover to the next treatment period. Severe laryngeal attacks were not treated in KONFIDENT

bAs Ekterly 300 mg is not a recommended dose for treatment of acute HAE attacks, these data are not shown herein.

Among 110 patients who experienced ≥1 HAE attack treated with Ekterly, mean age was 38 years, 60% were female, and 84% were White

Of the 264 treated HAE attacks, 54% had peripheral symptoms only, 32% had abdominal symptoms only, 10% had abdominal and peripheral symptoms, 3% had mild to moderate laryngeal symptoms, and 1% had missing attack location

Key endpoint:
  • Primary endpoint was the time to beginning of symptom relief, defined as at least “a little better” at 2 consecutive time points within 12 hours of first dose administration, assessed using PGI-C (7-point scale, ranging from “much worse” to “much better”)
  • Median time to onset of symptom relief within 12 hours of first dose: 2.0 hours for Ekterly 600 mg vs “could not be estimated” for placebo (because <50% of group achieved this endpoint)
  • 76% of Ekterly 600 mg group vs 49% of placebo group achieved primary endpoint

Additional endpoints:
  • Time to first incidence of reduction in severity at 2 consecutive time points within 12 hours of first dose, assessed using PGI-S (5-point scale from “none” to “severe”): 9.1 hours for Ekterly 600 mg vs “could not be estimated” for placebo (because <50% of group achieved this endpoint)
  • Time to attack resolution, defined as PGI-S of “none” within 24 hours of first dose was significantly faster for Ekterly 600 mg vs placebo (though median times “could not be estimated” for either group because <50% of patients in each group achieved this endpoint)
  • Median time to onset of at least “better” at 2 consecutive time points on the PGI-C within 12 hours of the first dose: 4.6 hours for Ekterly 600 mg
<b>Generic</b> (danazol)<sup>7</sup>:
Efficacy of danazol established in HAE clinical trials not provided
in prescribing information
<b>Cinryze</b> (C1-INH [human] IV)<sup>8:
Efficacy of Cinryze demonstrated in:
  • Randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, crossover trial of 24 adult or adolescent patients 9-73 years of age with HAE and history of ≥2 attacks per month
  • Randomized, dose-ranging crossover trial of 12 pediatric patients 7-11 years of age

Adult/Adolescent Trial

Patients received Cinryze or placebo every 3 or 4 days, ~2 times per week. In this trial, patients received Cinryze for 12 weeks followed by placebo for 12 weeks; or placebo for 12 weeks followed by Cinryze for 12 weeks. A total of 22 patients were included in efficacy analysis

Key endpoint:
  • Mean number of attacks: 6.1 for Cinryze vs 12.7 for placebo

Additional endpoints:
  • Mean severity of attacks (on 3-point scale from “mild” to “severe”): 1.3 for Cinryze vs 1.9 for placebo
  • Mean attack duration: 2.1 days for Cinryze vs 3.4 days for placebo
  • Days of swelling: 10.1 days for Cinryze vs 29.6 days for placebo

Pediatric Trial

After an observation period, pediatric patients received Cinryze 500 IU or 1000 IU every 3-4 days for 12 weeks.

Key endpoint:
  • Mean number of attacks per month: 1.2 for Cinryze 500 IU and 0.7 for Cinryze 1000 IU vs 3.7 for placebo during observation

Additional endpoints:
  • Mean absolute reduction in number of attacks vs baseline: −2.6 for Cinryze 500 IU and −3.0 for Cinryze 1000 IU (71.1% and 84.5% mean reductions vs baseline, respectively)
<b>Haegarda</b> (C1-INH [human] SC)<sup>:
Efficacy of Haegarda demonstrated in:
  • Randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled,

crossover trial of 90 adult and adolescent patients with symptomatic HAE-C1INH-Type1 or -Type2 (Study 1)
  • Randomized, open-label, active treatment–controlled trial of 120 adult and pediatric patients with symptomatic HAE-C1INH-Type1 or -Type2 (Study 2)

Study 1

Median age was 40 years (range, 12-72) and 60 patients were female

Patients received Haegarda 60 IU/kg (n=45) or 40 IU/kgc (n=45) in one 16-week treatment period and then placebo in the other 16-week treatment period. Patients self-administered Haegarda or placebo 2 times per week. Efficacy was evaluated for the last 14 weeks of each period

cAs Haegarda 40 IU/kg is not a recommended dose for routine prophylaxis to prevent HAE attacks, these data are not shown herein.

Key endpoint:
  • Median number of attacks per month: 0.3 for Haegarda 60 IU/kg vs 3.8 for placebo, for median percentage reduction relative to placebo of 95%

Additional endpoints:
  • Proportion of patients attack-free through study: 40% on Haegarda 60 IU/kg
  • Mean number of rescue medication uses per month: 0.3 for Haegarda 60 IU/kg vs 3.9 for placebo

Study 2

Eligible patients from Study 1 could enroll in Study 2 for up to 140 weeks. 49.2% of patients in Study 2 were in Study 1

Median patient age was 41.0 years. Patients with monthly attack rate of 4.3 in the 3 months before study entry were enrolled and treated for mean of 1.4 years (maximum >2.5 years)

Endpoints:

For patients treated with Haegarda 60 IU/kg:
  • Proportion of patients with <1 attack monthly: 86.9%
  • Median annualized attack rate: 1.0
  • Median annualized rescue medication use: 0.0 times
  • Proportion of patients attack-free on study: 44.3%
<b>Takhzyro</b> (lanadelumab-flyo)<sup>1:
Efficacy of Takhzyro demonstrated in a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, parallel-group trial of 125 adult and pediatric patients ≥12 years of age with HAE-C1INH-Type1 or -Type2 and ≥1 attack per 4 weeks during the run-in period (Trial 1)

Patients who completed Trial 1 were eligible to rollover into an uncontrolled OLE study (Trial 2)

Trial 1

Patients received

lanadelumab-flyo 150 mg Q4W (n=28), 300 mg Q4W (n=29), 300 mg Q2W (n=27), or placebo (n=41) for 26 weeks. Patients ≥18 years of age were required to discontinue other HAE prophylaxis; all patients could use rescue medications to treat breakthrough attacks

Key endpoint:
  • Monthly attack rate from Day 0 to Day 182 (least squares mean reduction in number of attacks per month relative to placebo): 76% for 150 mg Q4W, 73% for 300 mg Q4W, and 87% for 300 mg Q2W

Additional endpoints:
  • Proportion of patients attack-free for 26-week treatment period: 44%, 31%, and 39% in 300 mg Q2W, 300 mg Q4W, and 150 mg Q4W groups respectively, vs 2% of placebo patients

Trial 2

All rollover patients

received a single dose of Takhzyro 300 mg and were followed until the first HAE attack occurred

At Week 4 post-dose, ~80% of patients who had been in 300 mg Q2W group (n=25) in Trial 1 remained attack-free
<b>Orladeyo</b> (berotralstat)<sup>11</s:
Efficacy of Orladeyo demonstrated in Part 1 of a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, parallel-group trial of 120 adult and adolescent patients ≥12 years of age with HAE-C1INH-Type1 or -Type2 and ≥2 attacks in 8-week run-in period (Trial 1)

Trial 1

Patients received berotralstat 110 mg (n=41), berotralstat 150 mg (n=40), or placebo (n=40; 1 patient randomized but not treated) once daily, with food for the 24-week treatment period (Part 1). Patients were required to discontinue other HAE prophylaxis but could use rescue medications to treat breakthrough attacks

Key endpoint:
  • Monthly attack rate over 24 weeks: 1.31 for Orladeyo 150 mg and 1.65 for Orladeyo 100 mg (44.2% and 30% rate reductions relative to placebo, respectively)

Additional endpoints:
  • Proportion of patients with ≥50% relative reduction in attacks compared with baseline: 58% for 150 mg and 51% for 110 mg vs 25% for placebo
  • Proportion of patients with ≥90% reduction in attacks compared with baseline: 23% for 150 mg and 10% for 110 mg vs 8% for placebo
  • Rate of moderate/severe attacks reduced by 40% and 10% in 150 mg and 110 mg groups, respectively, vs placebo
<b>Andembry</b> (garadacimab-gxii)<sup>1:
Efficacy of Andembry demonstrated in 6-month, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, parallel-group trial of 64 adult and pediatric patients ≥12 years of age with HAE-C1INH-Type1 or -Type2 and ≥2 HAE attacks in 2-month period prior to study (VANGUARD)

VANGUARD

Mean age was 41 years, 59% were female, and 86% were White

Patients received Andembry (400 mg loading dose, then 200 mg once a month) or placebo. Patients were required to discontinue other HAE prophylaxis, but could use rescue medication to treat breakthrough attacks

Key endpoint:
  • Monthly attack rate at 6 months (least squares mean reduction in number of attacks per month): 0.22 vs 2.07 (89% reduction relative to placebo)

Additional endpoints:
  • Monthly rate of attacks requiring rescue medication: 91.2% reduction with Andembry relative to placebo
  • Monthly rate of moderate or severe attacks: 93.6% reduction with Andembry relative to placebo

  • Proportion of patients attack-free through 6-month treatment period compared to 2-month period prior to treatment: 62% on Andembry vs 0% on placebo
<b>Dawnzera</b><sup>TM</sup> (donidalors:
Efficacy of Dawnzera demonstrated

in a 24-week, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial of 90 adult and pediatric patients ≥12 years of age with HAE-C1INH-Type1 or -Type2, who had ≥2 attacks during 8-week run-in period (OASIS-HAE)

OASIS-HAE

Among 90 patients, mean age was 37 years, 53% were female, and 91% were White

Patients received Dawnzera 80 mg Q4W (n=45), Dawnzera 80 mg Q8W (n=23), or placebo (n=22). Patients were required to discontinue

other HAE prophylaxis (except androgens and tranexamic acid) but could use rescue medications to treat breakthrough attacks

Key endpoint:
  • Monthly attack rate from Week 0 to Week 24: 0.44 for Dawnzera Q4W and 1.02 for Dawnzera Q8W vs 2.26 for placebo (81% and 55% reductions vs placebo, respectively)

Additional endpoints:
  • Moderate or severe

attack rate from Week 4 to Week 24: 0.12 for Dawnzera Q4W and 0.68 for Dawnzera Q8W vs 1.15 for placebo (89% and 41% reductions vs placebo, respectively)
  • Attacks requiring acute therapy: 0.15 for Dawnzera Q4W and 0.59 for Dawnzera Q8W vs 1.80 for placebo (92% and 67% reductions vs placebo, respectively)
  • Proportion of patients attack-free Week 4 to Week 24: 53% for Dawnzera Q4W, 35% for Dawnzera Q8W, and 9% for placebo
